title-group.article-title: Letter to the Editor regarding: ‘Global epidemiology of diabetes and prediabetes in lean or non-obese patients with NAFLD: a systematic review and meta-analysis’

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Stomatology, Sanming Integrated Medicine Hospital, Sanming, China
bSchool of Artificial Intelligence in Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shanghai, China
cDepartment of Product Design, Sanming University, Sanming, China

fn.p: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

funding-group.funding-statement: The authors reported there is no funding associated with this work.

body.p: We read the systematic review and meta-analysis by Li et al. [1] with great interest. It states the global prevalence of diabetes (15.6%) and prediabetes (22.9%) in lean or non-obese patients with NAFLD. The research offers meaningful information on these overlooked individuals and indicates significant limitations relating to risks based on obesity. However, more discussion is needed about several methodologies.

First, the substantial variability observed (I2 = 100.0%) could be ascribed to heterogeneity in BMI cutoff criteria among studies. Different BMI cut-offs were used for Asian (<23 kg/m2) and non-Asian (<25 kg/m2) populations to define ‘lean’ NAFLD. Recent evidence indicates that the optimal BMI cutoffs may even vary within the Asian populations by ethnicity [2]. Our recommendation for future meta-analyses: conduct sensitivity analyses that stratify results by baseline BMI cutoff used and not solely based on geography. This could therefore help distinguish biology from definitional variation and result in more accurate estimates for developing clinical guidelines.

Second, the difference of NAFLD diagnosis methods needs attention. Although the authors performed a subgroup analysis according to diagnostic modality, they used ultrasonography in 29 studies, which may only have limited sensitivity in detecting hepatic steatosis less than 20–30% [3]. This means that lean persons with less severe disease may be underdiagnosed. On the contrary, liver biopsy can cause selection bias since it is usually done in suspected advanced disease. We recommend that future studies use controlled attenuation parameter (CAP) or magnetic resonance imaging-proton density fat fraction (MRI-PDFF), which have better accuracy and reproducibility in all categories of body mass index (BMI). Harmonizing diagnostic practices would greatly strengthen the evidence base.

Third, reporting of the severity of liver fibrosis is limited to varying degrees. The insufficient data prevented the authors from analysing such outcomes as non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), advanced fibrosis and HCC. However, the prevalence and clinical significance of diabetes might differ by fibrosis stage. It has been noted that lean NAFLD patients with diabetes progress to advanced fibrosis faster [4], which is why we suggest future research systematically reports fibrosis stage with either FIB-4 score or NAFLD fibrosis score or transient elastography. More clinically meaningful risk stratification might be enabled.

Fourth, the trend seen over time should be interpreted carefully. According to the findings, the prevalence of diabetes was estimated to be only 4.9% during pre-2010 and increased to 16.4% after 2010, that is when HbA1c was adopted for the diagnosis of diabetes. Nevertheless, citing these disparities may also reflect growing global prevalence of diabetes, awareness of lean NAFLD, or publication bias. For future analysis, we recommend meta-regression using midpoint year as a continuous variable and to specifically assess the diagnostic criteria used in each study.

In summary, Li et al. have provided important evidence supporting routine diabetes and prediabetes screening in lean or non-obese NAFLD patients. By standardizing BMI definitions and improving diagnostic accuracy, systematically reporting fibrosis staging, and refining time slope analysis, these methodological considerations would enhance future studies and ultimately provide improved risk estimation in this population.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: Provenance and peer review: Commentary, internally reviewed. Chenglu Ruan: conceptualization, methodology, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing; Lin Wang: conceptualization, methodology, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data sharing does not apply to this article, as no data were created or analysed in this study.

ref.mixed-citation: Li YY, Yu Y, Jing HB, et al. Global epidemiology of diabetes and prediabetes in lean or non-obese patients with NAFLD: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Med. 2026;58(1):2602995. doi: 10.1080/07853890.2025.2602995.41424121 PMC12724282

Wang S, Shen J, Koh WP, et al. Comparison of race- and ethnicity-specific BMI cutoffs for categorizing obesity severity: a multicountry prospective cohort study. Obesity. 2024;32(10):1958–1966. doi: 10.1002/oby.23928.39223976 PMC11421961

Hernaez R, Lazo M, Bonekamp S, et al. Diagnostic accuracy and reliability of ultrasonography for the detection of fatty liver: a meta-analysis. Hepatology. 2011;54(3):1082–1090. doi: 10.1002/hep.24452.21618575 PMC4197002

Younes R, Govaere O, Petta S, et al. Caucasian lean subjects with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease share long-term prognosis of non-lean: time for reappraisal of BMI-driven approach? Gut. 2022;71(2):382–390. doi: 10.1136/gutjnl-2020-322673.33541866
